_comment: Canonical cross-platform agent-logic parity vectors. The SAME input must produce the SAME AgentDecision / safety verdict on iOS (apple/.../AgentParityTests.swift) and Android (android/.../CoreVerify.kt). Each vector carries a stable `id`; both platform suites must tag the matching assertion with a `parity:<id>` marker comment. scripts/check_agent_parity.py enforces the bijection (every id covered on BOTH platforms, no orphans) so a future edit can't add a case to one platform and silently skip the other — the exact drift that produced 8 of the 11 bugs in docs/audits/2026-06-26-cross-platform-correctness-audit.md. The script checks COVERAGE (which ids are tested); each platform's own assertion checks CORRECTNESS (the expected value below). Keep input/expected here authoritative; source files encode them per-language (Swift #"..." raw vs Kotlin escaped).

vectors.note: Bare JSON tool call parses to a useTool decision.
A JSON answer embedded in prose is extracted.
H13: two JSON objects — take the FIRST complete one; never merge or borrow the 2nd's key (prompt-injection guard).
Keys nested inside another object are invisible (top-level-only read): the top-level tool wins; a buried answer is never fabricated.
tool/answer appearing ONLY nested → no decision (plan error), never a fabricated tool call.
No JSON object present → no decision.
\uXXXX must decode (café ☺). Android's hand-rolled unescaper rendered 'cafu00e9' before it learned \u; iOS JSONDecoder always did.
Short escapes \t \n must decode to a real tab/newline. Kotlin pinned this; Swift previously did not (asymmetry fixed alongside the guard).
M9: substrings of keywords (re*pay*, opi*nion*, bank*) must NOT trip a word-boundary blocklist.
An accented letter adjacent to a keyword makes a DIFFERENT word → no block. ICU \b does this natively; Java \b needs the (?U) flag.
Genuine dangerous actions MUST block on both platforms.
A plan array parses to a plan decision (count + first tool). Milestone 3.
STRICT: one item without a nonempty tool invalidates the WHOLE plan (never execute a half-parsed plan). Empty plans are nil too.
Precedence answer > plan > tool, identical on both platforms.

AUTHORITATIVE plan: a non-object member invalidates the WHOLE plan and NEVER falls through to the top-level tool. Swift used to run useTool(fallback), Kotlin used to run Plan([a]) — same output, two different tool executions (twin-drift audit agent-loop P1).
A plan of primitives is a malformed plan → nil on both; the top-level tool must NOT execute. Swift used to run useTool(calc) while Android halted with PLAN_ERROR (twin-drift audit agent-loop P2).

UnitConverter.format rounds half AWAY FROM ZERO on both platforms. Kotlin's Math.round is floor(x+0.5) — half toward +∞ — so -0.00025 rendered "-0.0002" on Android only (twin-drift audit agent-session P2). kotlin.math.round would be half-to-even — also wrong; the fix is definitional floor/ceil.

The calculator's non-integral observation uses ONE canonical rendering on both twins: 12 significant digits, HALF-EVEN ties (C %.11e on iOS; BigDecimal HALF_EVEN on Android — probed to agree incl. the 1234567890.125 tie), plain decimal for exponents -5..14 (so 0.1+0.2 reads "0.3" everywhere), canonical sci outside. Platform toString diverged in digits AND shape ("1.18…e+21" vs "1.18…E21"; twin-drift audit agent-session P2).

vectors.expected: Can't convert ² m to ft — they measure different things.
vectors.note: Digit scans use category Nd on both (Kotlin isDigit(); Swift isDecimalDigit). Swift's Character.isNumber also matched superscripts/fractions/Roman numerals, so "5²" swallowed the ² into the number on iOS and the two platforms answered differently (twin-drift audit agent-session P3).
